RESEARCH SUMMARY: THE EFFECTS OF SLEEP DEPRIVATION ON COGNITIVE PERFORMANCE
AND DECISION-MAKING IN KNOWLEDGE WORKERS

Published: Journal of Occupational Neuroscience, Vol. 18, Issue 3, 2024
Authors: Dr. Maya Patel, Dr. Jonathan Rivera, Dr. Soo-Yeon Kim
Institution: Stanford Sleep Research Center, Stanford University
DOI: 10.1109/JON.2024.0418

=============================================================================
ABSTRACT
=============================================================================

This study investigated the relationship between chronic partial sleep
deprivation and cognitive performance metrics in 342 knowledge workers over a
12-week period. Participants who averaged fewer than 6 hours of sleep per night
demonstrated a 34% reduction in executive function scores, a 28% decrease in
working memory capacity, and a 41% increase in decision-making errors compared
to the well-rested control group (7–9 hours per night). Notably, participants
consistently underestimated their own impairment level, with self-reported
performance scores averaging 18 percentage points higher than objective test
results. These findings have significant implications for workplace productivity
policies, particularly in high-stakes industries.

=============================================================================
1. INTRODUCTION
=============================================================================

Sleep is a fundamental biological requirement for cognitive function. The
National Sleep Foundation recommends 7 to 9 hours of sleep per night for adults
aged 18 to 64. Despite this, a 2023 CDC survey found that 35.2% of American
adults regularly sleep fewer than 7 hours per night. Among knowledge workers —
those whose primary work involves creating, processing, or communicating
information — rates of chronic sleep restriction are even higher, estimated at
42% according to a 2022 Gallup workplace survey.

Prior research has established links between acute sleep deprivation (defined
as 24+ hours without sleep) and severe cognitive impairment. However, the
cumulative effects of chronic partial sleep deprivation (CPSD), defined in this
study as consistently sleeping 5–6 hours per night, have been less studied in
naturalistic workplace settings.

This study aimed to: (1) quantify the specific cognitive domains most affected
by CPSD, (2) measure the gap between perceived and actual performance, and (3)
assess whether caffeine consumption can adequately offset CPSD-related deficits.

=============================================================================
2. METHODOLOGY
=============================================================================

2.1 Participants
342 knowledge workers (software engineers, financial analysts, medical
professionals, and marketing strategists) were recruited via LinkedIn and
professional associations. Mean age was 34.2 years (SD = 7.1). 54% identified
as male, 44% as female, 2% as non-binary. All participants were employed
full-time (40+ hours/week) and reported no pre-existing sleep disorders.

2.2 Sleep Measurement
Sleep duration was objectively measured using Fitbit Charge 5 wristbands worn
continuously for the 12-week study period. Self-reported sleep estimates were
collected weekly via a mobile survey. Participants were divided into three
groups:
  - Control Group    : 7–9 hours/night (n = 114)
  - Mild CPSD Group  : 6–7 hours/night (n = 112)
  - Severe CPSD Group: < 6 hours/night (n = 116)

2.3 Cognitive Assessments
Participants completed cognitive assessments every two weeks using the
Cambridge Neuropsychological Test Automated Battery (CANTAB), measuring:
  - Executive Function (Spatial Working Memory, Intra-Extra Dimensional Set Shift)
  - Working Memory Capacity (Spatial Span, Pattern Recognition Memory)
  - Decision-Making Accuracy (Cambridge Gambling Task, Iowa Gambling Task)
  - Sustained Attention (Rapid Visual Information Processing)
  - Processing Speed (Simple and Choice Reaction Time)

2.4 Caffeine Consumption Tracking
Daily caffeine intake was logged by participants via the mobile app. Doses were
categorized as low (< 200 mg/day), moderate (200–400 mg/day), and high (> 400
mg/day). The average daily caffeine intake across all participants was 287 mg.

=============================================================================
3. RESULTS
=============================================================================

3.1 Executive Function
Severe CPSD participants showed a 34% reduction in composite executive function
scores compared to the control group (p < 0.001). The Mild CPSD group showed
a 14% reduction (p < 0.01). Deficits were most pronounced in tasks requiring
task-switching and response inhibition, which are critical for complex
problem-solving and project management.

3.2 Working Memory
Working memory capacity in the Severe CPSD group decreased by 28% from
baseline over the 12-week period (p < 0.001). Interestingly, the Mild CPSD
group showed a 9% reduction, suggesting a non-linear relationship between
sleep restriction severity and working memory impairment.

3.3 Decision-Making Accuracy
The most striking finding was a 41% increase in decision-making errors in
the Severe CPSD group (p < 0.001), with errors concentrated in high-uncertainty
decision scenarios. The Iowa Gambling Task results showed that sleep-deprived
participants were significantly more likely to favor short-term rewards over
long-term optimal strategies.

3.4 The Performance Perception Gap
A critical finding was the "metacognitive blindspot": participants in the
Severe CPSD group self-rated their performance an average of 18 percentage
points higher than their objective test scores. This overconfidence effect
increased progressively over the 12-week period, suggesting that prolonged
sleep deprivation impairs not just performance, but also the ability to
accurately self-assess that performance.

3.5 Caffeine as a Mitigating Factor
High caffeine consumption (> 400 mg/day) partially offset attention and
processing speed deficits in the Mild CPSD group (+11% improvement vs.
low caffeine users in the same group). However, caffeine showed no significant
mitigating effect on executive function or decision-making accuracy in the
Severe CPSD group. This suggests that caffeine can restore alertness but
cannot compensate for deep cognitive impairments caused by severe sleep loss.

=============================================================================
4. DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS
=============================================================================

4.1 Key Conclusions
  1. Chronic partial sleep deprivation significantly impairs executive function
     (-34%), working memory (-28%), and decision-making accuracy (+41% errors).
  2. Sleep-deprived workers dramatically overestimate their own performance,
     creating a dangerous "metacognitive blindspot" in workplace settings.
  3. Caffeine restores surface alertness but does NOT compensate for deep
     cognitive deficits in severely sleep-deprived individuals.
  4. Even mild sleep restriction (6–7 hrs) produces measurable cognitive
     deficits of 9–14%, relevant for safety-critical decisions.

4.2 Implications
  - Organizations should consider sleep health as an occupational safety issue
  - High-stakes decisions (e.g., surgery, financial trading, code review) should
    not be scheduled during known circadian low points (2–4 AM, early afternoon)
  - Meeting fatigue and after-hours communications significantly worsen CPSD

4.3 Limitations
  - Sample was self-selected (LinkedIn) and skewed toward tech workers
  - Fitbit sleep tracking has ±15 min accuracy vs. polysomnography
  - Study did not control for individual chronotype (morning vs. evening types)

=============================================================================
FUNDING: This study was funded by the National Institutes of Health (NIH)
Grant R01HL145099 and a research gift from the Wellbeing Research Foundation.

CONFLICTS OF INTEREST: The authors declare no competing interests.
=============================================================================
END OF DOCUMENT
=============================================================================
